original_abstract:
Characterizing the elemental composition of organic aerosol (OA) is essential for understanding its sources, chemical transformation, and overall environmental impact. The Aerodyne high-resolution time-of-flight aerosol mass spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) has become a primary tool for these measurements. In this study, we re-evaluate AMS metrics—including oxygen-to-carbon (O:C), hydrogen-to-carbon (H:C), and organic mass-to-organic carbon (OM:OC) ratios, as well as carbon oxidation state ($\text{OS}_\text{C}$)—using a significantly expanded laboratory dataset of multifunctional organic standards. 

We found that the "Aiken-Explicit" method, which utilizes all measured ion intensities, captures O:C and H:C ratios within 20% and 12% of known values, respectively. However, the more standard "Aiken-Ambient" approach—which estimates $\text{H}_2\text{O}^+$ and $\text{CO}^+$ intensities to avoid atmospheric interferences—tends to systematically underestimate these ratios, particularly for alcohols and organic acids. Our analysis of $\text{H}_2\text{O}^+$, $\text{CO}^+$, and $\text{CO}_2^+$ fragments, supported by combined AMS and vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) ionization experiments, reveals that these ions primarily result from dehydration and decarboxylation on the $600^\circ\text{C}$ AMS vaporizer. Notably, this thermal decomposition remains efficient even at temperatures as low as $200^\circ\text{C}$.

To address these systematic biases, we propose an "Improved-Ambient" elemental analysis method. By using specific ion fragments as markers for molecular functionality, this method reduces errors and provides a more accurate representation of complex organic mixtures. When applied to ambient OOA, the Improved-Ambient method yields O:C and H:C ratios that are 27% and 11% higher, respectively, than those derived from previous methods. These findings suggest that ambient aerosols are more oxygenated than previously thought. Interestingly, $\text{OS}_\text{C}$ values remained consistent across methods, identifying it as a more robust metric for tracking OA oxidation since it is inherently less sensitive to the effects of water loss or gain during atmospheric processing or instrumental analysis.
humanized_abstract:
Determining the elemental composition of organic aerosol (OA) is a pivotal step toward understanding the sources, chemistry, and impacts of OA. The majority of these studies currently employ the Aerodyne high-resolution time-of-flight aerosol mass spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS). Building on previous work, here we re-examine the oxygen-to-carbon (O:C), hydrogen-to-carbon (H:C), and organic mass-to-organic carbon (OM:OC) ratios and the carbon oxidation state (OSC) metric using a much larger suite of multifunctional organic acids and bases.

We examine the effect of different ionization and analytical procedures on quantifying specific functional groups in biomass. The “Aiken-Explicit” approach utilizes all ion signals from the vaporizer, and estimates O:C and H:C on the order of 20% and 12% accuracy, respectively. In contrast, the “Aiken-Ambient” method which includes the estimation of $\text{H}_2\text{O}^+$ and $\text{CO}^+$ signals in order to reduce interference from atmospheric sources, tends to systematically undercount O:C and H:C in certain organic compounds such as alcohols and organic acids. A closer look at $\text{H}_2\text{O}^+$, $\text{CO}^+$ and $\text{CO}_2^+$ has been carried out through combined analysis using an aerosol mass spectrometer (AMS) and vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) ionization, with the results suggesting that dehydration and decarboxylation reactions happen predominantly in the $600^\circ\text{C}$ vaporizer, but occur to a smaller extent even at $200^\circ\text{C}$.

Currently, all elemental analysis methods employed for assessing OA composition are inherently subject to various systematic biases that, when taken together, lead to deviations from true elemental composition. We introduce the Improved-Ambient method for determining the elemental composition of ambient OA through employing specific ion fragments as markers for molecular functional groups, thereby eliminating some of the sources of error and allowing for a more accurate representation of the composition of complex organic mixtures. Applying the Improved-Ambient method to ambient OOA yields O:C and H:C ratios that are 27 % and 11 % higher than those obtained using prior methods. Thus, ambient aerosol appears to be more oxygenated than previously estimated. Importantly, $\text{OS}_\text{C}$ remained invariant between methods, implying that $\text{OS}_\text{C}$ can serve as the preferred metric for tracking the evolution of OA oxidation, as this metric is less susceptible to changes in $\text{OS}_\text{C}$ arising from atmospheric processing (e.g. hydration or dehydration) or from instrumental artifacts (e.g. adsorption or absorption of water).

document.output:

document.input:

The Aerodyne high-resolution time-of-flight aerosol mass spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) has become a primary tool for these measurements.
